content.text: Scorecard Review Cadence and Action Planning

A scorecard that is computed but never reviewed is a dashboard no one watches. The review cadence determines when people look at the scorecard, what decisions they make based on it, and who is responsible for following through. The cadence has three levels: a weekly automated report that requires no meeting, a sprint-level team review that surfaces trends and assigns action items, and a quarterly engineering leadership review that identifies systemic issues requiring formal remediation.

attrs.alt: Diagram: three-level scorecard review cadence — weekly automated, sprint team review, quarterly leadership review — and their respective triggers and audiences

content.text: Level 1 — Weekly Automated Report
What it does
The weekly scorecard is computed automatically every Monday morning and posted to the team's Slack channel. No meeting is required unless the status is Amber or Red. The automated report:
Computes quality and reliability scores from CI results and monitoring dashboards.
Compares the current score to the prior week and flags metrics that changed by more than 5 points.
Posts a summary to #scorecard with current status, dimension scores, and any changed metrics.
If any dimension is Amber or Red: tags the team lead and creates a GitHub issue automatically.
If status is Green and unchanged from prior week: posts a single-line 'All green' confirmation with no action required.
Slack message format

📊 Claude Scorecard — invoice-assistant — W26 2025

Overall: 🟡 AMBER (83.2)

  Quality:       🟢 GREEN (91.5)   ↔ (was 91.0)
  Reliability:   🟡 AMBER (78.0)   ↓ (was 81.0)
  Process:       🟢 GREEN (88.0)   ↑ (was 84.0)
  Cost:          🟡 AMBER (69.0)   ↓ (was 73.0)
  Security:      🟢 GREEN (100.0)  ↔

Changed metrics this week:
  ⬇ Reliability: P95 latency at 82% of SLA (was 76%)
  ⬇ Cost: cost_per_request at 108% of budget (was 94%) ← RED

Action items created:
  → GH #1247: [Amber] P95 latency approaching SLA — @alice
  → GH #1248: [Red] Cost/request exceeds budget — @bob

No meeting required if your team is already tracking these issues.
For questions, ping #prompt-oncall.
Level 2 — Sprint Review
Cadence and participants

At the end of each sprint, the team reviews the scorecard trend for the last four sprints (one month of history). This review is 15 minutes in the sprint retrospective, not a separate meeting. Participants: the full feature team including the PM.
What is reviewed
The four-sprint trend chart for each dimension — which dimensions are improving, stable, or declining.
Each open GitHub issue created from prior scorecard Amber/Red findings — was the action item completed? Did the metric move?
Any metric that has been Amber for two or more consecutive sprints — this indicates a structural issue that sprint-level action has not resolved.
The combined status for this sprint — is the team ready for the quarterly review, or are there outstanding remediation plans to close?
Action item creation in the sprint review
Every Amber or Red metric that is not already tracked by an open GitHub issue must receive one before the sprint review ends. The action item format:

Title: [Scorecard Amber] Reliability: P95 latency at 82% of SLA

Metric: p95_latency_pct_of_sla
Current value: 0.82 (amber threshold: 0.70)
Sprint trend: 0.76 → 0.78 → 0.80 → 0.82 (3-sprint rising trend)

Root cause hypothesis:
Context injection grew from ~3k to ~5k tokens per request after the
invoice line item retrieval expansion in W23. Input token size increase
directly correlates with latency rise.

Owner: @alice
Target: Reduce P95 to < 70% of SLA by end of next sprint
Plan:
  1. Profile context builder to confirm token growth hypothesis
  2. Apply slice cap to invoice line items (max 5 instead of unbounded)
  3. Re-measure P95 after context cap is applied in staging

An action item without a root cause hypothesis and a specific plan is a placeholder, not a commitment. The sprint review owner must present at least a hypothesis — 'we don't know yet' is not acceptable for a metric that has been Amber for two sprints.
Level 3 — Quarterly Engineering Review
Cadence and participants

The quarterly engineering review occurs at the end of each quarter and covers the full 13-sprint scorecard history for every Claude integration team. Participants: team leads, engineering managers, and the prompt lead. Senior leadership (CTO, VP Engineering) are briefed on a summary slide deck rather than attending the full review.
What is reviewed
Review item
Who presents
Decision required
13-sprint trend for each dimension, each team
Team leads (3 min per team)
No decision required unless declining trend
Teams with sustained Amber (3+ sprints in any dimension)
Team lead + EM
Formal remediation plan with target date and EM approval
Teams with any Red in the quarter
Team lead + EM + security reviewer (if security Red)
Post-mortem presented; remediation plan approved or feature deprioritised
Target calibration: are the current Healthy/Amber/Red thresholds still appropriate?
Prompt lead
Threshold updates to scorecard config for next quarter
Cross-team patterns: are multiple teams amber on the same metric?
Engineering manager
Shared tooling or process improvement proposed
Formal remediation plans
A team presenting a sustained Amber or any Red at the quarterly review must have a formal remediation plan. Verbal commitments are not accepted. The plan document:

## Remediation Plan — Reliability: P95 Latency
Team: invoice-assistant
Metric: p95_latency_pct_of_sla
Current value: 0.88 (amber)
Duration of amber status: 4 sprints (W22–W25)

Root cause (confirmed):
Invoice line item context injection grew from 3k to 6k tokens per request
following the expanded retrieval feature in W22. The latency increase tracks
the token count increase 1:1.

Plan:
Sprint W26: Apply context cap (invoice lines to max 5 items)
Sprint W27: Profile remaining latency: is streaming needed?
Sprint W28: Target value ≤ 0.70 (Green threshold)

Validation: P95 latency measured weekly; plan is complete when 2
consecutive Green readings are recorded.

Owner: Alice (tech lead)
EM approval: Bob (engineering manager)
Date approved: 2025-07-01
Handling Scorecard Disagreements
When a team disputes a metric

Teams sometimes disagree with a scorecard metric — typically when they believe the measurement methodology is flawed or the threshold is miscalibrated for their integration. The process for raising a dispute:
The team raises the dispute in the weekly scorecard Slack thread with a specific technical objection (not 'this score is unfair' but 'this metric is inflated because of X').

If the objection is about methodology (e.g., P95 latency is being measured including an upstream Anthropic outage that skewed the numbers), the scorecard administrator can mark the affected sprint's data as anomalous and recompute with the outlier removed.

If the objection is about threshold calibration (e.g., the team's integration is a batch offline tool where P95 latency is irrelevant), they raise it at the quarterly target calibration review. Thresholds are updated quarterly, not sprint-by-sprint, to prevent gaming.
If the team believes a Red classification is incorrect, they still must present a root-cause analysis — if the metric is wrong, proving it is wrong is the root-cause analysis.
Scorecard Automation Checklist
For the cadence to work without manual overhead, the data collection and reporting must be fully automated. Verify each item before enabling the scorecard for a new team:
CI eval gate results are written to a known artifact path:
the scorecard script can read them without parsing CI logs.
Monitoring dashboard exports are available via API or scheduled export:
P95 latency, error rate, and availability can be pulled programmatically.
GitHub issue creation is configured with the correct labels and assignees:
the Slack bot knows which team lead to tag for which integration.
Scorecard history is committed to the repository:
the quarterly review can pull 13 weeks of history from git rather than from a spreadsheet.
The Slack webhook is tested:
verify the Monday morning post arrives before relying on it for a real amber/red notification.
The sprint review calendar event includes the scorecard link:
teams that do not know where to find the scorecard will skip the review.
Scorecard Action Item Lifecycle
From Slack notification to closed issue

A scorecard action item begins when the weekly report flags an Amber or Red metric and ends when the metric returns to Green for two consecutive weeks. The lifecycle has defined states so that no action item falls through the cracks:
State
Trigger
Owner action
SLA
Open
Scorecard creates GitHub issue on Amber/Red
Assign root-cause hypothesis within 2 business days
2 business days
In investigation
Owner acknowledges and begins root-cause analysis
Post initial findings as comment; update status
End of current sprint
Plan approved
Owner presents plan in sprint review; EM approves
Execute plan; report progress weekly
Target date in plan
Resolved (pending verification)
Fix deployed; metric improving
Monitor for 2 consecutive Green readings
2 sprints
Closed
2 consecutive weekly Green readings recorded
Close GitHub issue with link to scorecard readings
Escalated
Open for > 5 business days without a plan, or plan failed
EM takes ownership; reassign or deprioritise
Immediate
Preventing action item accumulation

The most common failure mode in scorecard programs is action item accumulation: the team creates issues for every Amber metric but never closes them, so the issue tracker fills with stale scorecard items. Preventing this:
Cap open scorecard issues:
a team should never have more than 3 open scorecard issues simultaneously. If a fourth metric goes Amber, the team must either close one existing issue or escalate to the EM to re-prioritise.
Close issues even if metric did not fully recover:
if a plan was executed and the metric moved from 0.88 (Red) to 0.78 (Amber), close the Red issue and open a new Amber one. An issue that tracks both phases conflates two different action items.
Review age in the sprint retrospective:
any scorecard issue older than 4 sprints without resolution is automatically escalated to the EM. The escalation should be encoded in the GitHub Actions automation, not left to manual tracking.
Running the Sprint Review — Step by Step
15-minute scorecard segment in the sprint retrospective
The sprint-level scorecard review is 15 minutes in the team retrospective. Run it from a shared screen showing the scorecard dashboard:

Pull up the four-sprint trend table for all five dimensions. Spend 2 minutes identifying which dimensions changed direction this sprint (a dimension that was Green and turned Amber, or Amber and turned Green).
For each dimension that changed: identify the specific metric that drove the change. One sentence per dimension. Do not deep-dive at this point.

Review open GitHub issues: for each open scorecard issue, confirm that the associated metric actually moved in the expected direction. If it did not move, the plan is not working — say that explicitly.
For any Amber metric with no open issue: create the GitHub issue now, assign an owner, and agree on a root-cause hypothesis. This takes 2 minutes per metric.
Confirm the overall combined status. If the status is Red: the EM is notified (if not already present), and the team commits to a root-cause presentation within 3 business days.
End: confirm who is responsible for which open issue and what their next check-in date is. Do not leave the retrospective without owners on every open action item.
Quarterly Review Preparation Guide
What to prepare before the quarterly review
The quarterly review requires more preparation than the sprint review. Each team lead should spend 1–2 hours preparing the following materials one week before the quarterly review:
13-sprint trend table:
a table with one row per dimension and 13 columns (one per sprint). Each cell contains the score and a green/amber/red colour indicator. Highlight sprints where the overall status was Red.
Notable events log:

a bullet list of events that caused significant score changes in the quarter — model updates from Anthropic, major feature launches, incidents, infrastructure changes. This contextualises the trend for reviewers who were not in the sprint reviews.
Remediation outcomes:
for each formal remediation plan opened during the quarter, report whether the target was met, partially met, or missed, and why.
Outstanding action items:
list all open GitHub scorecard issues at the time of the review, their age, and current status.
Threshold calibration proposal (if any):
if the team believes a threshold is miscalibrated for their integration, prepare a data-backed argument (not just 'the threshold is too strict').
Quarterly review output
The quarterly review produces three outputs:
Updated threshold configurations for the next quarter (if any thresholds are changed, all teams are notified one sprint in advance).
Formal remediation plans approved for any team with sustained Amber or Red — signed off by EM and prompt lead.
Shared engineering initiatives for cross-team patterns: if three teams are all Amber on P95 latency, there may be a shared infrastructure improvement that addresses all three simultaneously.
Scorecard for Executive Briefing
Engineering leadership and executives need a different view of the scorecard than the team does. The executive briefing (quarterly, 5 minutes) presents:
Briefing item
Format
Source
Fleet health summary: how many integrations are Green / Amber / Red this quarter?
Single table, one row per team
Current scorecard status for all active Claude integrations
Trend direction: is the overall fleet health improving quarter-on-quarter?
Simple line chart: % Green teams over last 4 quarters
Scorecard history aggregated across all teams
Security posture: are all injection probes passing across all teams?
Single number: % of templates with 100% probe pass rate
Quality scorecard security dimension
Open remediation plans: how many teams have formal remediation plans, and what is the EM's confidence level in each?
One sentence per plan, RAG status
EM assessment

Executive briefings on scorecard health should not contain individual metric numbers — they contain status (Green / Amber / Red), trend direction, and decision points. A briefing that lists P95 latency values for each team is a data dump, not a briefing. Lead with the status, follow with the trend, end with what decisions are needed.
Common Cadence Failures and How to Fix Them
Scorecard cadence programs break in predictable ways. Recognising the pattern early avoids letting the program degrade to a vanity dashboard:
Failure pattern
How it manifests
Root cause
Fix
The weekly report is ignored
Scorecard posts land in Slack with no reactions; no GitHub issues created
Green status becomes normalised; team stops reading Amber notifications
Add an explicit agreement: the team lead reads the scorecard every Monday and responds in thread, even if only 'all good'
Action items are created but never closed
GitHub issue tracker has 20+ open scorecard issues, oldest from 6 months ago
No one reviews age; owners change teams; plans were vague
Cap open issues at 3; auto-escalate after 4 sprints; require explicit closure comment
Sprint review skips the scorecard
Team runs out of time; scorecard segment is deferred
Retrospective is running over; scorecard feels optional
Time-box to 15 minutes and make it the first agenda item, not the last
Quarterly review produces no decisions
Teams present trends; no formal plans are written; same issues appear next quarter
Review is informational only; EM does not enforce plan creation
Require a written remediation plan for each sustained Amber before the quarterly ends; EM signs off
Thresholds are never updated
Thresholds set at program launch are still in use after 2 years; some metrics are perpetually Green or perpetually Red
Calibration review is skipped; 'it's been working fine'
Make threshold calibration a mandatory agenda item at every quarterly review
Integrating the Scorecard with OKRs
Scorecard metrics as OKR key results

Engineering scorecards and OKRs (Objectives and Key Results) are complementary. Scorecard metrics are operational health indicators; OKRs express aspirational improvement targets. For a Claude integration team, the connection:
Objective: Improve the quality of the invoice assistant integration.
Key result: Eval pass rate consistently above 92% by end of Q3 (currently at 87%, scorecard Amber).
Objective: Eliminate production reliability incidents from the integration.
Key result: P95 latency below 70% of SLA for 8 consecutive weeks (currently at 82%, scorecard Amber).
Objective: Make every Claude integration security-auditable.
Key result: 100% of templates on current safety baseline version, with security reviewer sign-off, by end of Q2.

OKRs set the quarterly direction; the scorecard tracks weekly progress. A team that sets 'eval pass rate above 92%' as a key result uses the weekly scorecard to see whether the metric is moving in the right direction each sprint, rather than finding out at the end of the quarter that they missed it.

The scorecard answers 'are we healthy right now?' The OKR answers 'where are we trying to get to?' When they are aligned, the scorecard's amber/red status for a metric is a direct signal that an OKR key result is at risk — actionable information rather than just a number.
Summary

The scorecard review cadence has three levels, each with a different audience and purpose: weekly automated reports that require no meeting unless status is Amber or Red; sprint reviews that surface trends and create concrete action items with named owners; and quarterly engineering reviews that handle sustained Amber cases and formal remediation plans. Action planning is not optional — every Amber or Red metric must have a GitHub issue with a root-cause hypothesis, an owner, and a target completion date before the sprint review ends.
Weekly automated reports are the primary notification mechanism — they post to Slack and create GitHub issues without requiring a meeting.
Sprint reviews focus on trend direction rather than the current score: a metric that has been Amber for three sprints with no improvement is a systemic issue, not a sprint-level task.
Quarterly reviews require formal remediation plans for sustained Amber and any Red — verbal commitments are not sufficient.
Disputes about metric methodology are handled via the scorecard administrator; threshold disputes are handled at the quarterly calibration review.
Full automation of data collection is a prerequisite: a scorecard that requires manual data entry will degrade within two sprints.
